Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'm a hands-on learner, so I spent a lot of my 32 hours per week doing practical exercises, building things, and working through problems physically rather than just reading. I'd block out time in the mornings when my mind was fresh, but I often skipped the online components in favor of working with physical materials or practicing techniques in person. The LMS was more of a reference point than my main study tool.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life. Being 26, I have responsibilities outside of school that younger students might not have, so some weeks I'd be locked in studying consistently, and other weeks I'd have to cram everything around work and family obligations. Deadlines definitely pushed me to be more structured, but otherwise I went with the flow based on my energy levels and what was demanding my attention.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I absolutely needed to check assignment requirements or see if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials that I could then work with offline. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS casually—it was more transactional for me, get in, get what I need, get out.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way checking email might be. I'm more of a tactile person, so sitting at a computer clicking through modules didn't feel like real learning to me. When I did use it regularly, it was usually because I was scared of missing something important, not because it felt natural.
Q5: The video content was actually helpful when I watched it—I logged over 200 minutes because some of those demonstrations showed techniques I could then practice myself. Anything that I could translate into hands-on practice felt worthwhile. The downloadable resources were also useful because I could print them out and mark them up physically while working through problems.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums—I only posted once the whole course. It felt awkward typing out my thoughts when I'd rather just talk to someone in person or work through problems by doing them. I also delayed a lot of the reading-heavy modules because sitting and reading on a screen drains me quickly. I knew I should engage more, but it just wasn't how I learn best.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to something practical or when it would help me pass the exam. If I could take what I learned and actually do something with it—apply it, build something, solve a real problem—then it was valuable. Abstract discussions or busy-work assignments that didn't connect to real skills felt like a waste.
Q8: Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I rarely got to because I was already stretched thin with the required work. Required activities had that urgency attached to them, that fear of failing if I didn't complete them. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never really came because something more pressing always took priority.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and videos, then work through them on my own without being connected to the LMS. I also used textbooks and external YouTube tutorials that explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me. My hands-on practice with actual materials and tools was probably where I learned the most, and none of that showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged in learning but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper, or I'd be in study groups with classmates where we'd work through concepts together physically. I also did a lot of mental rehearsal and reflection during commutes or while doing other tasks—that's real learning, but it's invisible to any system.
Q11: Yes, this was my main approach actually. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then return to it multiple times offline over the following days or weeks. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times on my own. It was more efficient for me to have everything saved locally where I could access it without internet issues.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear and stress made it harder to engage. I'd feel scared about falling behind or not understanding something, and that anxiety would sometimes paralyze me instead of motivating me. When multiple deadlines piled up or when personal responsibilities got intense, I'd withdraw from the LMS and just try to survive rather than thrive. The mindfulness exercises they offered actually helped me manage those moments.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed with competing responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd cut back on. I'd prioritize the bare minimum—checking what was due, submitting what I could—but I wouldn't engage deeply with the content. Confusion also played a role; when I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how behind I felt.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. My eyes would be on the screen but my mind was elsewhere—worried about other things or just too tired to process anything. I'd technically complete the activity but retain almost nothing. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was going through the motions to check a box rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was fear—I was scared of doing them wrong or not understanding the material well enough. So I'd avoid them until the pressure became unbearable. It's a bad cycle: the more I postponed, the more scared I got, and the harder it became to start. That's probably why my assignment completion was so low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have periods of almost no engagement, then a spike of activity right before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but the fear of failing or missing a deadline was often the only thing strong enough to overcome my avoidance. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less with the online components.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always in the way it should have. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me more scared and avoidant. I'd delay looking at grades or comments because I was afraid of what they'd say. When I did receive constructive feedback, I tried to apply it, but the emotional weight of criticism was hard for me to shake off quickly.
Q18: The structure of the course assumed a lot of online engagement, which didn't match my learning style at all. I'm kinesthetic—I need to move, touch, practice. Sitting through online modules felt like fighting against my nature. The 20 online courses I was juggling made it even harder to give any single one the attention it deserved. A more flexible structure with hands-on options would have helped me engage better.
Q19: The heavy reliance on digital materials and online discussions made engagement harder for me. I would have thrived with more practical assignments, labs, or in-person components. On the positive side, having materials available to download was helpful because I could take them offline. But the overall design assumed students learn best by reading and clicking, which isn't true for everyone.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are genuinely trying but struggling to complete things to a standard they're comfortable submitting. They might be exploring, watching videos, reading—but then get stuck when it comes to actually producing work. Fear of failure could be a factor too. You're engaging because you want to learn, but you're too scared or overwhelmed to submit work that might be judged.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I performed reasonably well on the exam despite low LMS activity because most of my real learning happened offline. I studied intensively using downloaded materials, physical practice, and external resources. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course content. Low activity doesn't mean low effort—it might just mean the effort is happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how long they were logged in. They can track submission patterns and identify students who might be completely disengaged. But they miss everything that happens offline—the reading, the practice, the discussions with classmates, the thinking. They also can't capture the quality of engagement or whether someone was actually focused during their logged time.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, trying to understand it and apply it. It's not just showing up or clicking through screens—it's that feeling of being mentally invested in figuring something out. For me, engagement often looks like hands-on practice, asking questions, and connecting concepts to real situations. It's about genuine curiosity and effort, not just presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent practicing skills physically, working through problems on paper, or discussing concepts with study partners in person. It wouldn't capture the emotional journey—the fear I felt, the mindfulness practices I used to manage my stress, or the relief when something finally clicked. My real engagement story is much richer than any log could represent, and I think that's true for a lot of students.
